blocks.text: Prepared for {{fact:f:E-2004-001.client}} by Charles Olson, Design Technician. This report covers the period since the last progress meeting.

The work is on schedule and roughly at its midpoint. The analysis is complete, the detention concept is fixed, and the plan set is in production. Nothing has occurred that changes the scope described in our engagement letter, and we do not presently foresee a request for additional services. The one item that could still move the schedule is the response time of the reviewing authority, which we do not control and never have.

Of the lump sum fee of {{fact:f:E-2004-001.fee}}, we have earned approximately fifty percent to date, which tracks the completion of the work. Our next invoice will reflect that figure. There are no additional services to report and no reimbursable costs outside the fee.

The geotechnical report reached this office and confirmed that the pipe crossing can be held above the water table. The single-basin arrangement your office preferred is therefore workable, and Pamela Warren confirmed it in writing as she said she would. The basin, the outlet structure, and the conveyance to it are sized. The outlet is a two-stage structure, which is what the ordinance drives us to when a site has to meet both a water quality volume and a peak rate.

The second discharge point is re-shot and the field note is in the file. It sits lower than the survey gave it, which is the third time on this site that the ground has been lower than the drawing, and it is the reason we walked the property before we computed anything. The number we designed to is our own.

Grading and drainage sheets are drafted to a first check. Jeffrey Perez has the details built off the fixed outlet structure and has begun the quantity takeoff. He checks a takeoff once and then checks it again from the other direction, which is slower than the alternative and cheaper than being wrong about it in a bid. The erosion and sedimentation control sheets are revised to show the limits at the improvements, with the note we discussed directing the contractor to enclose any disturbance he adds.

Pamela Warren will draft the stormwater management report with the computations behind it and the assumptions stated where the reviewer can find them. The plan set goes to internal check and then to the Principal Engineer, who reads every sheet before it is sealed. The sealed package, plan set and report together, goes to the reviewing authority, and we will transmit it on your behalf and confirm receipt. We will then answer the first round of comments in writing, in the order the authority issues them.

Between now and the seal the remaining items are the report narrative, the second check of the takeoff, the cover sheet, and the internal review. None of them depends on information we do not have. That is worth saying plainly, because it has not been true on this job until this period, and it is the reason we are reporting the work on schedule rather than reporting a date and hoping.

Review time. The authority's comments arrive when they arrive. We have built the schedule on the response time we have seen from that office in the past, and if it runs long the submittal date is unaffected but the approval date moves. We will tell you the day we know.

The receiving ditch. It is shallow, it is silted, and it is not on ground you control. Our design discharges to it at a rate the ordinance permits and our obligation ends at the property line. But a reviewer who drives out and looks at that ditch may ask a question about it, and the answer will take a letter and possibly a meeting. It is not a design problem. It is a schedule problem, and it has been in the file since we walked the site.

Late changes. The grading follows the building footprint. If the footprint moves, the runoff moves, and the pond moves with it. We asked that any change be sent to this office before it is final and we ask it again here. A change that arrives after the seal goes on is a resubmittal, and a resubmittal is additional services under the letter.

Questions on this report should be directed to Pamela Warren, who has charge of the design, or to me on the field items. We will contact your office when the package is ready to transmit. Johnson PLC will have a copy of everything that goes to the authority on the day it goes.
